title: On Some Conjectures of Samuels and Feige
overall_summary: The paper studies independent nonnegative random variables X_1,...,X_n with E X_i = 1 and a normalized weighted sum Z = ∑ μ_i X_i, where μ_1 ≥ ... ≥ μ_n > 0 and ∑ μ_i = 1. Its main result is conditional: Samuels' product-form conjectural lower bound on P(Z < 1+δ) implies Feige's simpler conjectural lower bound P(Z < 1+δ) ≥ min(δ/(δ+M), 1/e), where M is the largest weight. The proof introduces a logarithmic transform Φ of the product factors, uses monotonicity of Φ in the normalized weight, and then uses concavity of Φ along rays to reduce a continuous minimization to endpoint cases. For the original Bernoulli-weighted-sum problem, the most transferable ingredients are the log-product normalization, the reduction of weight-vector dependence to largest-weight and endpoint configurations, and the sharp two-point extremal examples, since scaled Bernoulli variables are also nonnegative two-point mean-one variables.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let n be a positive integer, let μ_1 ≥ ... ≥ μ_n > 0 with μ_1 + ... + μ_n = 1, let X_1,...,X_n be independent nonnegative random variables with E X_i = 1 for every i, and set Z = ∑_{i=1}^n μ_i X_i. Let M = μ_1, let δ > 0, and let T = 1 + δ. The theorem states that the universal validity of Samuels' product lower bound in this setting implies the universal validity of Feige's lower bound in this setting. Explicitly, if for all such data one has P(Z < T) ≥ min_{1≤i≤n} ∏_{j=1}^i (1 − μ_j/(T − ∑_{k=i+1}^n μ_k)), then for all such data one has P(Z < T) ≥ min(δ/(δ+M), 1/e).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume the Samuels product bound. Define partial sums σ_i = ∑_{k=1}^i μ_k. Since T − ∑_{k=i+1}^n μ_k = 1+δ − (1−σ_i) = σ_i + δ, Samuels gives P(Z < 1+δ) ≥ min_i ∏_{j=1}^i (1 − μ_j/(σ_i+δ)). Choose an index i attaining the minimum. Introduce Φ on [0,1] × (0,∞) by Φ(μ,ρ) = μ^{-1} log(1 − μ/(1+ρ)) for μ > 0 and Φ(0,ρ) = −1/(1+ρ). For j ≤ i, the identity Φ(μ_j/σ_i, δ/σ_i) = (σ_i/μ_j) log(1 − μ_j/(σ_i+δ)) rewrites the logarithm of the product as ∑_{j=1}^i (μ_j/σ_i) Φ(μ_j/σ_i, δ/σ_i). The coefficients μ_j/σ_i sum to 1. Since μ_j ≤ M and Lemma 3.2 says Φ is decreasing in its first variable, each term Φ(μ_j/σ_i, δ/σ_i) is at least Φ(M/σ_i, δ/σ_i), hence log P(Z < 1+δ) ≥ Φ(M/σ_i, δ/σ_i). Now M ≤ σ_i ≤ 1, so the right side is bounded below by min_{σ∈[M,1]} Φ(M/σ, δ/σ). Put t = M/σ; then t ranges over [M,1] and the expression becomes Φ(t, (δ/M)t). Lemma 3.4 says t ↦ Φ(t, (δ/M)t) is concave, so its minimum on [M,1] occurs at an endpoint. Therefore log P(Z < 1+δ) ≥ min(Φ(1, δ/M), Φ(M,δ)) = min(log(δ/(δ+M)), (1/M) log(1 − M/(1+δ))). Finally Lemma 3.3, applied with μ = M and ρ = δ, gives exp(Φ(M,δ)) ≥ min(δ/(δ+M), 1/e). Combining this with the exact value of Φ(1,δ/M) yields P(Z < 1+δ) ≥ min(δ/(δ+M), 1/e).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every real t in the interval [0,1], one has 1 − t/(1 + t/(e−1)) ≥ e^{−t}, where e is the base of the natural logarithm.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The desired inequality is rearranged as 1 − e^{−t} ≥ t/(1+t/(e−1)), or equivalently f(t) ≥ 0 for f(t) = (1 + t/(e−1))(1 − e^{−t}) − t. The paper computes f(0)=0, f'(t) = (1/(e−1)) e^{−t}((e−2)(1−e^t)+t), and f''(t) = (1/(e−1)) e^{−t}(3−e−t). Thus f is convex on [0,3−e] and concave on [3−e,1]. Since f'(0)=0, convexity gives that f is increasing on [0,3−e], so f(3−e) ≥ f(0)=0. On [3−e,1], concavity and the endpoint facts f(3−e) ≥ 0 and f(1)=0 imply f(t) ≥ 0 throughout that interval. Hence f(t) ≥ 0 for all t∈[0,1].

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Define Φ: [0,1] × (0,∞) → R by Φ(μ,ρ) = μ^{-1} log(1 − μ/(1+ρ)) when μ∈(0,1], and Φ(0,ρ) = −1/(1+ρ). If ρ > 0 and λ, μ ∈ [0,1] satisfy λ < μ, then Φ(μ,ρ) < Φ(λ,ρ). In other words, for fixed positive ρ, Φ is strictly decreasing in its first argument.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Define φ on [0,1) by φ(s) = −∑_{k≥0} s^k/(k+1), equivalently φ(s)=log(1−s)/s for s∈(0,1) and φ(0)=−1. The power series has strictly negative coefficients in the sense that as s increases each nonconstant term makes the value smaller, so φ is strictly decreasing on [0,1). For μ∈[0,1], one can write Φ(μ,ρ) = (1/(1+ρ)) φ(μ/(1+ρ)), with the μ=0 case matching by definition. Since ρ>0, the argument μ/(1+ρ) lies in [0,1), and λ<μ implies λ/(1+ρ)<μ/(1+ρ). The strict decrease of φ gives Φ(μ,ρ)<Φ(λ,ρ).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Product-to-Φ averaging estimate used in the proof of Theorem 1.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let a_1,...,a_i be positive real numbers, let σ = a_1+...+a_i, let M = max_{1≤j≤i} a_j, and let δ>0. With Φ defined by Φ(u,r)=u^{-1} log(1−u/(1+r)) for u>0 and Φ(0,r)=−1/(1+r), one has log ∏_{j=1}^i (1 − a_j/(σ+δ)) = ∑_{j=1}^i (a_j/σ) Φ(a_j/σ, δ/σ) ≥ Φ(M/σ, δ/σ).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equality is obtained by substituting u=a_j/σ and r=δ/σ into the definition of Φ: multiplying Φ(a_j/σ,δ/σ) by a_j/σ gives log(1−a_j/(σ+δ)). The numbers a_j/σ are nonnegative and sum to 1. Since a_j/σ ≤ M/σ for every j, Lemma 3.2 gives Φ(a_j/σ,δ/σ) ≥ Φ(M/σ,δ/σ). Therefore their weighted average is at least Φ(M/σ,δ/σ). In the paper this is applied with a_j=μ_j for j≤i and σ=σ_i.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: With Φ: [0,1] × (0,∞) → R defined by Φ(μ,ρ)=μ^{-1} log(1−μ/(1+ρ)) for μ>0 and Φ(0,ρ)=−1/(1+ρ), for every μ∈[0,1] and every ρ>0 one has Φ(μ,ρ) ≥ log(min(ρ/(μ+ρ), 1/e)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The endpoint μ=0 is immediate because Φ(0,ρ)=−1/(1+ρ)>−1=log(1/e), while min(ρ/(0+ρ),1/e)=1/e. For μ=1, Φ(1,ρ)=log(ρ/(1+ρ))=log(ρ/(μ+ρ)), which is at least the logarithm of the stated minimum. Now fix μ∈(0,1). The paper notes that Φ is smooth and that its partial derivative in ρ is ∂_2 Φ(μ,ρ)=1/((1+ρ)(1+ρ−μ))>0, so Φ increases with ρ. First suppose ρ ≥ μ/(e−1). Then ρ/(μ+ρ) ≥ 1/e, so it suffices to show Φ(μ,ρ) ≥ −1. By monotonicity in ρ, Φ(μ,ρ) ≥ Φ(μ, μ/(e−1)); Lemma 3.1 with t=μ gives 1 − μ/(1+μ/(e−1)) ≥ e^{−μ}, hence Φ(μ, μ/(e−1)) ≥ −1. Second suppose 0<ρ≤μ/(e−1). Then ρ/(μ+ρ)≤1/e, so the desired bound is Φ(μ,ρ) ≥ log(ρ/(μ+ρ)). Let g(ρ)=Φ(μ,ρ)−log(ρ/(μ+ρ)). The derivative computed in the paper is g'(ρ)= −(1−μ)((1+ρ)μ−ρ^2)/(ρ(1+ρ)(1+ρ−μ)(μ+ρ)). Since ρ≤μ/(e−1)<μ, the factor ((1+ρ)μ−ρ^2) is positive, so g'(ρ)<0 on this interval. Thus g(ρ) ≥ g(μ/(e−1)). At ρ=μ/(e−1), the ratio ρ/(μ+ρ) equals 1/e, so g(μ/(e−1))=Φ(μ, μ/(e−1))+1≥0 by the first case. This proves the bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let α>0. With Φ defined by Φ(μ,ρ)=μ^{-1} log(1−μ/(1+ρ)) for μ>0 and Φ(0,ρ)=−1/(1+ρ), the function h_α given on (0,1] by h_α(t)=Φ(t,αt) and extended continuously to h_α(0)=−1 is concave on [0,1].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For t∈(0,1), h_α(t)=t^{-1} log(1 − t/(1+αt)). It is enough to show h_α''(t)≤0 on (0,1), since the endpoint values are handled by continuity. The paper defines η(t)=t^3 h_α''(t) and computes η(t)= t(2+(6α−3)t+4(α^2−α)t^2)/((1−t+αt)^2(1+αt)^2) + 2 log(1 − t/(1+αt)). Differentiating gives η'(t)= − t^2(1 + 3(1−2α+2(α−α^2)t)^2)/(2(1−t+αt)^3(1+αt)^3). For α>0 and t∈(0,1), the denominator is positive and the numerator is positive, so η'(t)<0. Since lim_{s→0} η(s)=0, η(t)<0 for all t∈(0,1). Therefore h_α''(t)=η(t)/t^3<0 on (0,1), proving concavity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Endpoint minimization consequence of Lemma 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M∈(0,1] and δ>0. With Φ as above, the paper uses the identity min_{σ∈[M,1]} Φ(M/σ, δ/σ) = min_{t∈[M,1]} Φ(t, (δ/M)t) = min(Φ(1,δ/M), Φ(M,δ)). Equivalently, this minimum is min(log(δ/(δ+M)), (1/M) log(1 − M/(1+δ))).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Substitute t=M/σ. As σ runs over [M,1], t runs over [M,1] with the order reversed, and δ/σ=(δ/M)t. Hence the minimization becomes that of h_{δ/M}(t)=Φ(t,(δ/M)t) over t∈[M,1]. By Lemma 3.4, h_{δ/M} is concave on [0,1], hence also on [M,1]. A concave function on a compact interval is bounded below by the smaller of its endpoint values, so the minimum over [M,1] is attained at t=M or t=1. Evaluating Φ at those endpoints gives Φ(1,δ/M)=log(δ/(δ+M)) and Φ(M,δ)=(1/M)log(1−M/(1+δ)).

proof_techniques.name: Logarithmic normalization of product bounds
proof_techniques.description: The proof converts Samuels' product ∏_{j=1}^i (1 − μ_j/(σ_i+δ)) into a sum of logarithms and then into a weighted average of values of the auxiliary function Φ. The normalization by σ_i is essential: the factors become Φ(μ_j/σ_i, δ/σ_i), and the coefficients μ_j/σ_i form a probability vector. This turns a multiplicative expression depending on many weights into an averaged analytic expression on normalized weights.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For weighted Bernoulli sums, probabilities of events such as all variables in a selected set failing or succeeding naturally produce products over weights or over Bernoulli probabilities. A similar log-and-normalize step could help reduce a complicated product or generating-function coefficient estimate to an inequality involving normalized weights.

proof_techniques.name: Collapse to the largest normalized weight via monotonicity
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.2 proves that Φ is strictly decreasing in its first argument. Once the logarithm of the product is written as ∑ (μ_j/σ_i) Φ(μ_j/σ_i,δ/σ_i), every term is bounded below by replacing μ_j/σ_i with the largest possible normalized weight M/σ_i. This removes all detailed dependence on μ_1,...,μ_i except through M and σ_i.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A proof of the original inequality may need to hold uniformly over all weight vectors. Establishing monotonicity of an appropriate transform with respect to individual weights could similarly reduce the search for worst cases to configurations governed by the maximum weight.

proof_techniques.name: Concavity along rays and endpoint reduction
proof_techniques.description: After the largest-weight reduction, the remaining unknown is the partial sum σ_i. The substitution t=M/σ_i makes the two arguments of Φ proportional: Φ(M/σ_i,δ/σ_i)=Φ(t,(δ/M)t). Lemma 3.4 proves concavity of this one-variable function, so the minimum over the whole interval is attained at endpoints. The endpoints correspond to σ_i=M and σ_i=1, i.e. the two extreme regimes recovered in Feige's bound.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This suggests looking for a one-parameter concavity principle in the Bernoulli problem that reduces arbitrary weight distributions to endpoint configurations, such as one dominant weight versus a fully spread or fully accumulated set of weights.

proof_techniques.name: Regime split at the 1/e crossing
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.3 compares Φ(μ,ρ) to log(min(ρ/(μ+ρ),1/e)) by splitting at ρ=μ/(e−1), exactly where ρ/(μ+ρ)=1/e. Above the crossing, monotonicity of Φ in ρ and the scalar inequality of Lemma 3.1 give the universal −1 lower bound. Below the crossing, the derivative of g(ρ)=Φ(μ,ρ)−log(ρ/(μ+ρ)) is negative, so the endpoint value at the crossing controls the interval.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the original problem leads to a comparison between a weight-dependent obstruction and a universal constant or threshold in p, this two-regime method gives a template: identify the crossing point, prove a universal bound on one side, and use derivative monotonicity to propagate it on the other.

proof_techniques.name: Two-point extremizers for mean-one nonnegative variables
proof_techniques.description: The paper records equality examples for the conjectured bounds in which each random variable is either 0 or a single compensating high value, sometimes with other variables fixed at 1. For Samuels' product, fixing variables after index i at 1 and making variables up to i two-point produces equality because Z<T occurs exactly when all first i variables take value 0. These examples show that product probabilities arise naturally from zero-or-large-value extremizers.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: After scaling by p, Bernoulli(p) variables become two-point nonnegative mean-one variables taking 0 and 1/p. Thus the extremal shapes emphasized in the paper are structurally close to the variables in the original problem, even though the paper's threshold is 1+δ rather than the boundary threshold 1.

proof_techniques.name: Sharpness analysis through largest-weight and many-small-weights examples
proof_techniques.description: Feige's conjectured lower bound has two different sharpness mechanisms in the introduction: a single largest random coordinate realizes the δ/(δ+M) term, while equal small weights with rare large values show that the universal constant cannot exceed 1/e. The proof of Theorem 1.1 mirrors this dichotomy through endpoint minimization.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the Bernoulli problem, this points to testing and possibly proving against a small set of extremal weight patterns, especially one large coordinate and equal-weight configurations. Such tests can help identify the correct range of p before pursuing a general-weight argument.

other_useful_info: The paper states but does not prove Samuels' conjecture; its main contribution is the implication from Samuels' conjecture to Feige's conjecture. The introduction records sharpness examples. For Feige's δ/(δ+M) term, if M=μ_1, X_2=...=X_n=1, and X_1 equals (δ+M)/M with probability M/(δ+M) and 0 with probability δ/(δ+M), then P(Z<T)=δ/(δ+M). For the universal constant, if μ_1=...=μ_n=1/n and each X_i equals n(1+δ) with probability 1/(n(1+δ)) and 0 otherwise, then P(Z<T)=(1−1/(n(1+δ)))^n; this family tends to exp(−1/(1+δ)) as n→∞ and approaches 1/e as δ↓0, showing no constant larger than 1/e can be valid in Feige's conjecture. For Samuels' product, equality for any fixed i is obtained by setting X_j=1 for j>i and, for j≤i, taking X_j=0 with probability 1−μ_j/D and X_j=D/μ_j with probability μ_j/D, where D=T−∑_{k=i+1}^n μ_k; then the event Z<T occurs exactly when all first i variables are zero, giving probability ∏_{j=1}^i (1−μ_j/D). The paper cites Feige [1,2] for the original result with constant 1/13 in place of 1/e and Samuels [3] for the product-form conjecture.
why_relevant: Studies weighted sums of independent nonnegative variables and the relationship between Samuels' and Feige's conjectures. The notation and extremal reductions closely match the scaled form of the present problem.
